NRSV.label: New Revised Standard Version
NRSV.rights: New Revised Standard Version Bible, copyright © 1989 National Council of the Churches of Christ in the United States of America. Used by permission. All rights reserved worldwide.

text.NRSV: The Song of Songs, which is Solomon’s.
text.Rotherham: The Song of Songs, which pertaineth to Solomon.

text.NRSV: Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth! For your love is better than wine,
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth! [THEY] For better are thy caresses than wine:

text.NRSV: your anointing oils are fragrant, your name is perfume poured out; therefore the maidens love you.
text.Rotherham: Like the fragrance of thy precious oils, Oil poured out, is thy name, For this cause, virgins love thee.

text.NRSV: Draw me after you, let us make haste. The king has brought me into his chambers. We will exult and rejoice in you; we will extol your love more than wine; rightly do they love you.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Draw me! [THEY] After thee, will we run! [SHE] The king, hath brought me, into his chambers. [THEY] We will exult and rejoice in thee, we will mention thy caresses, beyond wine, Sincerely they love thee.

text.NRSV: I am black and beautiful, O daughters of Jerusalem, like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Solomon.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Swarthy, I am but comely, ye daughters of Jerusalem. [THEY] Like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Solomon.

text.NRSV: Do not gaze at me because I am dark, because the sun has gazed on me. My mother’s sons were angry with me; they made me keeper of the vineyards, but my own vineyard I have not kept!
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Do not look on me, because, I, am so swarthy, because the sun hath scorched me,—My mother’s sons, were angry with me, they set me to keep the vineyards, Mine own vineyard, have I not kept. …

text.NRSV: Tell me, you whom my soul loves, where you pasture your flock, where you make it lie down at noon; for why should I be like one who is veiled beside the flocks of your companions?
text.Rotherham: Tell me, thou loved of my soul! Where wilt thou pasture thy flock? Where wilt thou let them recline at noon? For why should I be as one that wrappeth a veil about her, by the flocks of thy companions?

text.NRSV: If you do not know, O fairest among women, follow the track of the flock, and pasture your kids beside the shepherds’ tents.
text.Rotherham: [HE] If thou know not of thyself, most beautiful among women! get thee forth in the footsteps of the flock, and pasture thy kids by the huts of the shepherds. …

text.NRSV: I compare you, my love, to a mare among Pharaoh’s chariots.
text.Rotherham: To a mare of mine, in the chariots of Pharaoh, have I likened thee, my fair one!

text.NRSV: Your cheeks are comely with ornaments, your neck with strings of jewels.
text.Rotherham: Comely are thy cheeks, with bead-rows, thy neck, with strings of gems.

text.NRSV: We will make you ornaments of gold, studded with silver.
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Rows of golden ornaments, will we make thee, with studs of silver.

text.NRSV: While the king was at his repose, my nard gave forth its fragrance.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] By the time the king is in his circle, my nard, will have given out its fragrance:

text.NRSV: My beloved is to me a bag of myrrh that lies between my breasts.
text.Rotherham: A bag of myrrh, is my beloved to me, between my breasts, shall it tarry the night!

text.NRSV: My beloved is to me a cluster of henna blossoms in the vineyards of En-gedi.
text.Rotherham: A cluster of henna, is my beloved to me, in the vineyards of En-gedi.

text.NRSV: Ah, you are beautiful, my love; ah, you are beautiful; your eyes are doves.
text.Rotherham: [HE] Lo! thou art beautiful my fair one, lo! thou art beautiful, Thine eyes, [are] doves!

text.NRSV: Ah, you are beautiful, my beloved, truly lovely. Our couch is green;
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Lo! thou art beautiful, my beloved, Yea delightful! [BOTH] Yea! our couch, is covered with leaves:

text.NRSV: the beams of our house are cedar, our rafters are pine.
text.Rotherham: The beams of our house, are cedars, Our fretted ceiling, is cypress-trees.

text.NRSV: I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] I am The meadow-saffron of Sharon, The lily of the valleys.

text.NRSV: As a lily among brambles, so is my love among maidens.
text.Rotherham: [HE] As a lily among thorns, So, is my fair one, among the daughters!

text.NRSV: As an apple tree among the trees of the wood, so is my beloved among young men. With great delight I sat in his shadow, and his fruit was sweet to my taste.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] As an apple-tree among the trees of the forest, So, is my beloved, among the sons: In his shade, I greatly delighted and sat down, And, his fruit, was sweet to my taste.

text.NRSV: He brought me to the banqueting house, and his banner over me was love.
text.Rotherham: He hath brought me into the house of wine, and, his banner over me, is love.

text.NRSV: Sustain me with raisins, refresh me with apples; for I am faint with love.
text.Rotherham: Sustain me with raisin-cakes, refresh me with apples,—for sick with love, I am.

text.NRSV: O that his left hand were under my head, and that his right hand embraced me!
text.Rotherham: His left hand under my head, then, his right hand, embraceth me!

text.NRSV: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or the wild does: do not stir up or awaken love until it is ready.
text.Rotherham: [HE] I adjure you, ye daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or by the hinds of the field,—That ye wake not, nor arouse, the dear love until she please! [****]

text.NRSV: The voice of my beloved! Look, he comes, leaping upon the mountains, bounding over the hills.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] The voice of my beloved! Lo! here he cometh,—leaping over the mountains, skipping over the hills.

text.NRSV: My beloved is like a gazelle or a young stag. Look, there he stands behind our wall, gazing in at the windows, looking through the lattice.
text.Rotherham: Resembleth, my beloved, a gazelle, or a young stag,—Lo! here he is, standing behind our wall, looking in at the windows, peeping in at the lattice.

text.NRSV: My beloved speaks and says to me: “Arise, my love, my fair one, and come away;
text.Rotherham: Responded my beloved, and said to me,—Rise up! my fair—my beautiful—one, and come away,

text.NRSV: for now the winter is past, the rain is over and gone.
text.Rotherham: For lo, the winter, is past,—the rain, is over, [and] gone;

text.NRSV: The flowers appear on the earth; the time of singing has come, and the voice of the turtle-dove is heard in our land.
text.Rotherham: The flowers, have appeared in the earth, the time of the spring-song, hath come,—and, the voice of the turtle, is heard in our land;

text.NRSV: The fig tree puts forth its figs, and the vines are in blossom; they give forth fragrance. Arise, my love, my fair one, and come away.
text.Rotherham: The fig-tree, hath spiced her green figs, and, the vines—all blossom, yield fragrance,—Rise up! my fair—my beautiful—one, and come away!

text.NRSV: O my dove, in the clefts of the rock, in the covert of the cliff, let me see your face, let me hear your voice; for your voice is sweet, and your face is lovely.
text.Rotherham: [HE] O my dove! In the retreats of the crag, in the hiding-place of the terrace, Let me see thy form, Let me hear thy voice,—For, thy voice, is sweet, and, thy form, comely.

text.NRSV: Catch us the foxes, the little foxes, that rob the vineyards— for our vineyards are in blossom.”
text.Rotherham: [BOTH] Take ye for us, the foxes, the little foxes that are spoiling the vines,—and, our vines, are all blossom!

text.NRSV: My beloved is mine and I am his; he pastures his flock among the lilies.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] My beloved, is, mine, and, I, am, his, he that pastureth among lilies!

text.NRSV: Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, turn, my beloved, be like a gazelle or a young stag on the cleft mountains.
text.Rotherham: Until the day, breathe, and the shadows, be lengthened, Again, liken thyself, my beloved, to a gazelle, or to a young stag, upon the cleft mountains. [****]

text.NRSV: Upon my bed at night I sought him whom my soul loves; I sought him, but found him not; I called him, but he gave no answer.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Upon my couch, in the night-time, sought I the beloved of my soul,—I sought him, but found him not.

text.NRSV: “I will rise now and go about the city, in the streets and in the squares; I will seek him whom my soul loves.” I sought him, but found him not.
text.Rotherham: Come! I must arise, and go about in the city, In the paths and in the broadways, I must seek the beloved of my soul,—I sought him, but found him not.

text.NRSV: The sentinels found me as they went about in the city. “Have you seen him whom my soul loves?”
text.Rotherham: The watchmen that go round in the city, found me, The beloved of my soul, have ye seen?

text.NRSV: Scarcely had I passed them when I found him whom my soul loves. I held him, and would not let him go until I brought him into my mother’s house, and into the chamber of her that conceived me.
text.Rotherham: Scarcely had I passed from them, when I found the beloved of my soul,—I caught him, and would not let him go, until that I had brought him into the house of my mother, and into the chamber of her that conceived me.

text.NRSV: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or the wild does: do not stir up or awaken love until it is ready.
text.Rotherham: [HE] I adjure you ye, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles, or by the hinds of the field,—That ye wake not, nor arouse, the dear love until she please. [****]

text.NRSV: What is that coming up from the wilderness, like a column of smoke, perfumed with myrrh and frankincense, with all the fragrant powders of the merchant?
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Who is this, coming up out of the wilderness, like pillars of smoke,—with perfume of myrrh, and frankincense, besides all the aromatic powder of the merchant?

text.NRSV: Look, it is the litter of Solomon! Around it are sixty mighty men of the mighty men of Israel,
text.Rotherham: Lo! his couch, ’tis Solomon’s own, Threescore heroes, around it,—of the heroes of Israel:

text.NRSV: all equipped with swords and expert in war, each with his sword at his thigh because of alarms by night.
text.Rotherham: All of them, grasping the sword, trained for war,—every man, with his sword upon his thigh, because of dread, in the night-time.

text.NRSV: King Solomon made himself a palanquin from the wood of Lebanon.
text.Rotherham: A palanquin, King Solomon made himself, of the trees of Lebanon:

text.NRSV: He made its posts of silver, its back of gold, its seat of purple; its interior was inlaid with love by the daughters of Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: The supports thereof, made he of silver, the couch thereof, of gold, the seat thereof, of purple,—the midst thereof, hath an inlay of love from the daughters of Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Come out, O daughters of Zion, and look at King Solomon, at the crown with which his mother crowned him on the day of his wedding, on the day of the gladness of his heart.
text.Rotherham: Go forth and gaze, ye daughters of Zion, upon King Solomon,—wearing the crown, wherewith his mother, crowned him, in the day of his marriage, and in the day of his heart gladness.

text.NRSV: How beautiful you are, my love, how very beautiful! Your eyes are doves behind your veil. Your hair is like a flock of goats, moving down the slopes of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: [HE] Lo! thou art beautiful, my fair one, Lo! thou art beautiful, Thine eyes, are doves, from behind thy veil,—Thy hair, is like a flock of goats, which are reclining on the sides of Mount Gilead:

text.NRSV: Your teeth are like a flock of shorn ewes that have come up from the washing, all of which bear twins, and not one among them is bereaved.
text.Rotherham: Thy teeth, are like a flock, evenly grown, which have come up from the washing-place,—whereof, all of them, are twin-bearers, and bereaved, is none among them:

text.NRSV: Your lips are like a crimson thread, and your mouth is lovely. Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.
text.Rotherham: Like a cord of crimson, are thy lips, and, thy mouth, is lovely,—Like a slice of pomegranate, are thy temples, behind thy veil:

text.NRSV: Your neck is like the tower of David, built in courses; on it hang a thousand bucklers, all of them shields of warriors.
text.Rotherham: Like the tower of David, is thy neck, built for war,—A thousand shields, hung thereon, all, equipment of heroes:

text.NRSV: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle, that feed among the lilies.
text.Rotherham: Thy two breasts, are like two young roes, twins of a gazelle,—which pasture among lilies.

text.NRSV: Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, I will hasten to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense.
text.Rotherham: Until the day, breathe, and the shadows, be lengthened, I will get me unto the mountain of myrrh, and unto the hill of frankincense.

text.NRSV: You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no flaw in you.
text.Rotherham: Thou art, all over, beautiful, my fair one, and, blemish, is there none in thee.

text.NRSV: Come with me from Lebanon, my bride; come with me from Lebanon. Depart from the peak of Amana, from the peak of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.
text.Rotherham: With me, from Lebanon, O bride, with me, from Lebanon, shalt thou enter,—Thou shalt look round from the top of Amana, from the top of Senir, and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.

text.NRSV: You have ravished my heart, my sister, my bride, you have ravished my heart with a glance of your eyes, with one jewel of your necklace.
text.Rotherham: Thou hast encouraged me, my sister, bride,—thou hast encouraged me, with one [glance] of thine eyes, with one ornament of thy neck.

text.NRSV: How sweet is your love, my sister, my bride! how much better is your love than wine, and the fragrance of your oils than any spice!
text.Rotherham: How beautiful are thy caresses, my sister, bride,—how much more delightful thy caresses, than wine, and the fragrance of thine oils, than all spices:

text.NRSV: Your lips distill nectar, my bride; honey and milk are under your tongue; the scent of your garments is like the scent of Lebanon.
text.Rotherham: With sweetness, thy lips do drip, O bride,—Honey and milk, are under thy tongue, and, the fragrance of thy garments, is like the fragrance of Lebanon.

text.NRSV: A garden locked is my sister, my bride, a garden locked, a fountain sealed.
text.Rotherham: A garden barred, is my sister, bride,—a spring barred, a fountain sealed:

text.NRSV: Your channel is an orchard of pomegranates with all choicest fruits, henna with nard,
text.Rotherham: Thy buddings forth, are a paradise of pomegranates, with precious fruits,—henna bushes, with nard blossoms:

text.NRSV: nard and saffron, calamus and cinnamon, with all trees of frankincense, myrrh and aloes, with all chief spices—
text.Rotherham: Nard and saffron, sweet cane and cinnamon, with all woods of frankincense,—myrrh and aloes, with all the chiefs of spices:

text.NRSV: a garden fountain, a well of living water, and flowing streams from Lebanon.
text.Rotherham: A garden fountain, a well of living waters,—and flowings from Lebanon.

text.NRSV: Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind! Blow upon my garden that its fragrance may be wafted abroad. Let my beloved come to his garden, and eat its choicest fruits.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Awake, O north wind, and come in, thou south, Fan my garden—its balsams, will flow out,—Let my beloved enter his garden, and eat his precious fruits.

text.NRSV: I come to my garden, my sister, my bride; I gather my myrrh with my spice, I eat my honeycomb with my honey, I drink my wine with my milk. Eat, friends, drink, and be drunk with love.
text.Rotherham: [HE] I have entered my garden, my sister, bride, I have plucked my myrrh, with my balsam, I have eaten the honey of my thicket, I have drunk my wine, with my milk:—Eat ye, O friends, Drink, yea drink abundantly, ye beloved! [****]

text.NRSV: I slept, but my heart was awake. Listen! my beloved is knocking. “Open to me, my sister, my love, my dove, my perfect one; for my head is wet with dew, my locks with the drops of the night.”
text.Rotherham: [SHE] I, was sleeping, but, my heart, was awake,—The voice of my beloved—knocking! Open to me, my sister, my fair one, my dove, my perfect one, for, my head, is filled with dew, my locks, with the moisture of the night.

text.NRSV: I had put off my garment; how could I put it on again? I had washed my feet; how could I sully them?
text.Rotherham: I have put off my tunic, oh how shall I put it on? I have bathed my feet, oh how shall I soil them?

text.NRSV: My beloved thrust his hand into the opening, and my inmost being yearned for him.
text.Rotherham: My beloved, thrust in his hand, at the window, and, my feelings, were deeply moved for him:

text.NRSV: I arose to open to my beloved, and my hands dripped with myrrh, my fingers with liquid myrrh, upon the handles of the bolt.
text.Rotherham: I myself, arose, to open to my beloved,—and, my hands, dripped with myrrh, and, my fingers, with myrrh distilling, upon the handles of the bolt.

text.NRSV: I opened to my beloved, but my beloved had turned and was gone. My soul failed me when he spoke. I sought him, but did not find him; I called him, but he gave no answer.
text.Rotherham: I myself, opened to my beloved, but, my beloved, had turned away, had passed on,—My soul, had gone out when he spake, I sought him, but found him not, I called him, but he answered not.

text.NRSV: The sentinels found me as they went about in the city; they beat me, they wounded me, they took away my mantle, those sentinels of the walls.
text.Rotherham: The watchmen who were going round in the city, found me, they smote me, wounded me,—The watchmen of the walls, took away my cloak from off me.

text.NRSV: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, if you find my beloved, tell him this: I am faint with love.
text.Rotherham: I adjure you, ye daughters of Jerusalem,—If ye find my beloved, what will ye tell him? That, sick with love, I am.

text.NRSV: What is your beloved more than another beloved, O fairest among women? What is your beloved more than another beloved, that you thus adjure us?
text.Rotherham: [DAUGHTERS OF JERUSALEM] What is thy beloved more than any other beloved, thou most beautiful among women? What is thy beloved more than any other beloved, that, thus, thou hast adjured us?

text.NRSV: My beloved is all radiant and ruddy, distinguished among ten thousand.
text.Rotherham: [SHE.] My beloved, is white and ruddy, conspicuous beyond ten thousand:

text.NRSV: His head is the finest gold; his locks are wavy, black as a raven.
text.Rotherham: His head, is pure gold,—his locks, are bushy, black as a raven;

text.NRSV: His eyes are like doves beside springs of water, bathed in milk, fitly set.
text.Rotherham: His eyes, like doves, by the channels of water,—bathing in milk, set as gems in a ring:

text.NRSV: His cheeks are like beds of spices, yielding fragrance. His lips are lilies, distilling liquid myrrh.
text.Rotherham: His cheeks, like a raised bed of balsam, growing plants of perfume,—His lips, lilies, dripping with myrrh distilling:

text.NRSV: His arms are rounded gold, set with beryl. His body is ivory work, encrusted with sapphires.
text.Rotherham: His hands, cylinders of gold, set with topaz,—His body, wrought work of ivory, covered with sapphires:

text.NRSV: His legs are phoenix-pillars of alabaster, set on bases of gold. His appearance is like Lebanon, choice as the cedars.
text.Rotherham: His legs, pillars of white marble, founded on sockets of gold,—His form, like Lebanon, choice as cedars:

text.NRSV: His speech is most sweet, and he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend, O daughters of Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: His mouth, most sweet, yea, altogether, he is delightful,—This, is my beloved, yea, this, is my dear one, ye daughters of Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Where has your beloved gone, O fairest among women? Which way has your beloved turned, that we may seek him with you?
text.Rotherham: [DAUGHTERS OF JERUSALEM] Whither hath thy beloved, gone, thou most beautiful among women? whither hath thy beloved turned him aside? That we may seek him with thee.

text.NRSV: My beloved has gone down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to pasture his flock in the gardens, and to gather lilies.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] My beloved, is gone down to his garden, to the beds of balsam,—to pasture in the gardens, and to gather lilies,

text.NRSV: I am my beloved’s and my beloved is mine; he pastures his flock among the lilies.
text.Rotherham: I, am, my beloved’s, and, my beloved, is mine, he that pastureth among lilies.

text.NRSV: You are beautiful as Tirzah, my love, comely as Jerusalem, terrible as an army with banners.
text.Rotherham: [HE] Beautiful, art thou, my fair one, as Tirzah, comely, as Jerusalem,—majestic as bannered hosts!

text.NRSV: Turn away your eyes from me, for they overwhelm me! Your hair is like a flock of goats, moving down the slopes of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: Turn away thine eyes from me, for, they, have excited me,—Thy hair, is like a flock of goats, that are reclining on the sides of Mount Gilead:

text.NRSV: Your teeth are like a flock of ewes, that have come up from the washing; all of them bear twins, and not one among them is bereaved.
text.Rotherham: Thy teeth, are like a flock of sheep which have come up from the washing-place,—whereof, all of them, are twin-bearers, and bereaved, is there none among them:

text.NRSV: Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.
text.Rotherham: Like a slice of pomegranate, are thy temples, from behind thy veil:

text.NRSV: There are sixty queens and eighty concubines, and maidens without number.
text.Rotherham: Threescore, are the queens, and, fourscore, are the concubines,—and, virgins, there are, without number.

text.NRSV: My dove, my perfect one, is the only one, the darling of her mother, flawless to her that bore her. The maidens saw her and called her happy; the queens and concubines also, and they praised her.
text.Rotherham: One alone, is my dove, my perfect one, one alone, was she to her mother, Pure, was she to her that bare her,—The daughters, have seen her, and pronounced her happy, Queens and concubines, and they have praised her. [****]

text.NRSV: “Who is this that looks forth like the dawn, fair as the moon, bright as the sun, terrible as an army with banners?”
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Who is this, that looketh forth like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, pure as the sun, majestic as bannered hosts?

text.NRSV: I went down to the nut orchard, to look at the blossoms of the valley, to see whether the vines had budded, whether the pomegranates were in bloom.
text.Rotherham: [HE] To the garden of nuts, I went down, to look at the fresh shoots of the ravine,—to see whether: had burst forth the vine, had blossomed the pomegranate:—

text.NRSV: Before I was aware, my fancy set me in a chariot beside my prince.
text.Rotherham: I know not [how it was], my soul, set for me the chariots of my willing people!

text.NRSV: Return, return, O Shulammite! Return, return, that we may look upon you. Why should you look upon the Shulammite, as upon a dance before two armies?
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Return, return, O Shulamite, Return, return, that we may look on thee! [SHE] What would ye look on in the Shulamite? [THEY] As it were the dance of a double camp…

text.NRSV: How graceful are your feet in sandals, O queenly maiden! Your rounded thighs are like jewels, the work of a master hand.
text.Rotherham: How beautiful, are thy feet in sandals, O daughter of a noble,—The curvings of thy hips, are like ornaments wrought by the hands of a skilled workman:

text.NRSV: Your navel is a rounded bowl that never lacks mixed wine. Your belly is a heap of wheat, encircled with lilies.
text.Rotherham: Thy navel, is a round bowl, may it not lack spiced wine! Thy body, a heap of wheat fenced about with lilies;

text.NRSV: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.
text.Rotherham: Thy two breasts, are like two young roes, the twins of a gazelle:

text.NRSV: Your neck is like an ivory tower. Your eyes are pools in Heshbon, by the gate of Bath-rabbim. Your nose is like a tower of Lebanon, overlooking Damascus.
text.Rotherham: Thy neck, is like a tower of ivory,—Thine eyes, are pools in Heshbon, by the gate of Bath-rabbim, Thy nose, is like the tower of Lebanon, which looketh towards Damascus:

text.NRSV: Your head crowns you like Carmel, and your flowing locks are like purple; a king is held captive in the tresses.
text.Rotherham: Thy head upon thee, is like Carmel, And, the hair of thy head, is like purple,—The king, is held captive by the ringlets!

text.NRSV: How fair and pleasant you are, O loved one, delectable maiden!
text.Rotherham: [HE] How beautiful, and how delightful, O dear love, for delights:

text.NRSV: You are stately as a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters.
text.Rotherham: This thy stature, is like to a palm-tree, and, thy breasts, are like clusters:

text.NRSV: I say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its branches. O may your breasts be like clusters of the vine, and the scent of your breath like apples,
text.Rotherham: I said, I will ascend the palm-tree, I will lay hold of its fruit stalks—Oh then, let thy breasts, I pray thee, be like vine-clusters, And, the fragrance of thy nose, like apples;

text.NRSV: and your kisses like the best wine that goes down smoothly, gliding over lips and teeth.
text.Rotherham: And, thy mouth, like good wine—[SHE] Flowing to my beloved smoothly, gliding over the lips of the sleeping.

text.NRSV: I am my beloved’s, and his desire is for me.
text.Rotherham: I, am my beloved’s, and, unto me, is his longing.

text.NRSV: Come, my beloved, let us go forth into the fields, and lodge in the villages;
text.Rotherham: Come, my beloved, Let us go forth into the country, Let us stay the night in the villages:

text.NRSV: let us go out early to the vineyards, and see whether the vines have budded, whether the grape blossoms have opened and the pomegranates are in bloom. There I will give you my love.
text.Rotherham: Let us get up early to the vineyards, Let us see whether the vine, hath burst forth, the blossom, hath opened, the pomegranates, have bloomed,—There, will I give my caresses to thee.

text.NRSV: The mandrakes give forth fragrance, and over our doors are all choice fruits, new and old, which I have laid up for you, O my beloved.
text.Rotherham: The love-apples, have given fragrance, and, at our openings, are all precious things, new and yet old,—O my beloved! I have treasured them up for thee.

text.NRSV: O that you were like a brother to me, who nursed at my mother’s breast! If I met you outside, I would kiss you, and no one would despise me.
text.Rotherham: Oh that thou hadst been a very brother to me, who had sucked the breasts of my own mother,—Had I found thee without, I had kissed thee, Yea, folk would not have despised me!

text.NRSV: I would lead you and bring you into the house of my mother, and into the chamber of her who conceived me. I would give you spiced wine to drink, the juice of my pomegranates.
text.Rotherham: I would have guided thee—brought thee into the house of my mother, Thou wouldst have instructed me,—I would have let thee drink of spiced wine, of the pressed-out juice of my pomegranate.

text.NRSV: O that his left hand were under my head, and that his right hand embraced me!
text.Rotherham: His left hand under my head, then, his right hand, embraceth me.

text.NRSV: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, do not stir up or awaken love until it is ready.
text.Rotherham: I [HE] adjure you, O ye daughters of Jerusalem,—Why will ye wake, and why will ye arouse the dear love until she please! [****]

text.NRSV: Who is that coming up from the wilderness, leaning upon her beloved? Under the apple tree I awakened you. There your mother was in labor with you; there she who bore you was in labor.
text.Rotherham: [THEY] Who is this, coming up out of the wilderness, leaning upon her beloved? [HE] Under the apple-tree, I roused thee, where thy mother, was in pain with thee, where she was in pain who gave thee birth!

text.NRSV: Set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm; for love is strong as death, passion fierce as the grave. Its flashes are flashes of fire, a raging flame.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Set me as a seal, upon thy heart, as a seal upon thine arm, For, mighty as death, is love, Exacting as hades, is jealousy,—The flames thereof, are flames of fire, The flash of Yah!

text.NRSV: Many waters cannot quench love, neither can floods drown it. If one offered for love all the wealth of one’s house, it would be utterly scorned.
text.Rotherham: Many waters, cannot quench love, nor shall, floods, overwhelm it,—If a man would give all the substance of his house, for love, they would, utterly despise, him. [****]

text.NRSV: We have a little sister, and she has no breasts. What shall we do for our sister, on the day when she is spoken for?
text.Rotherham: [THEY] A sister, have we, a little one, and, breasts, hath she none, What shall we do for our sister, in the day when she may be spoken for?

text.NRSV: If she is a wall, we will build upon her a battlement of silver; but if she is a door, we will enclose her with boards of cedar.
text.Rotherham: If, a wall, she is, we will build upon it a battlement of silver,—but if, a door, she is, we will close it up with a plank of cedar.

text.NRSV: I was a wall, and my breasts were like towers; then I was in his eyes as one who brings peace.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] I, was a wall, and, my breasts, like towers,—Then, became I, in his eyes, one who did indeed find good content.

text.NRSV: Solomon had a vineyard at Baal-hamon; he entrusted the vineyard to keepers; each one was to bring for its fruit a thousand pieces of silver.
text.Rotherham: A vineyard, had Solomon, as the owner of abundance, He put out the vineyard to keepers,—Every man, was to bring in, for the fruit thereof, a thousand silverlings:

text.NRSV: My vineyard, my very own, is for myself; you, O Solomon, may have the thousand, and the keepers of the fruit two hundred!
text.Rotherham: Mine own vineyard, is before me,—The thousand belong to thee, O Solomon, and two hundred to the keepers of the fruit thereof.

text.NRSV: O you who dwell in the gardens, my companions are listening for your voice; let me hear it.
text.Rotherham: [HE] O thou fair dweller in the gardens, the companions are giving heed to thy voice, Let me hear it.

text.NRSV: Make haste, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices.
text.Rotherham: [SHE] Come quickly, my beloved, and resemble thou a gazelle, or a young stag, upon the mountains of balsam-trees.
